revision.comment: Bot: mark 5 deleted QID(s) as DELETED_QID in ill templates [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24147211140|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:كرات الاستحمام الفوارة]][[de:Badekugel]][[en:Bath bomb]][[es:Bomba de baño]][[id:Sabun bom mandi]][[it:Bomba da bagno]][[ja:入浴剤]][[jv:Bom adus]][[ko:배스 밤]][[nl:Badparel]][[simple:Bath bomb]][[sv:Badbomb]]
[[File:Lush_bath_bombs.jpg|thumb|right]] '''Nyūyokuzai''' (入浴剤) are substances added to bathwater during {{ill|Bathing|en|Bathing|qid=Q327651|lt=bathing|1=Bathing}} in a {{ill|Furo|en|Furo|qid=Q2637646|lt=bathtub|1=Furo}}. These are prepared bath products, such as fragrant bath salts, intended for {{ill|Bathing|en|Bathing|qid=Q327651|lt=bathing|1=Bathing}}.<ref name="osaka">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.customs.go.jp/osaka/toukei/pdf/tokushu_201004.pdf|title=入浴剤の輸入|publisher=大阪税関|accessdate=2019-10-20|qq=}}</ref> They come in various forms, including {{ill|Solid|en|Solid|qid=Q11438|lt=solids|1=Solid}}, {{ill|Powder|en|Powder|qid=Q2908004|lt=powders|1=Powder}} and {{ill|Liquid|en|Liquid|qid=Q11435|lt=liquids|1=Liquid}}. Bath additives can be broadly categorized into three types: those derived from natural {{ill|Plant|en|Plant|qid=Q756|lt=plants|1=Plant}} or {{ill|Chinese herbology|en|Chinese herbology|qid=Q261503|lt=herbal medicines|1=Chinese herbology}}, extracts of {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|qid=Q655311|lt=hot spring water|1=Onsen}} components, and inorganic {{ill|mineral salts|de|Mineralsalze|ja|無機塩類|zh|无机盐类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mineral salts|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミネラル|1=Mineral (nutrient)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q189598}} compounds. Combinations of these types are also available. In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, bath additives are regulated as either {{ill|Cosmetics|en|Cosmetics|qid=Q131207|lt=cosmetics|1=Cosmetics}} (bathing cosmetics), {{ill|quasi-drugs|de|Arzneimittel in der Übergangsphase|ja|医薬部外品|zh|准药品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/quasi-drugs|1=quasi-drug|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11404705}}, or {{ill|Medication|en|Medication|qid=Q12140|lt=pharmaceuticals|1=Medication}}. Most products on the market are classified as quasi-drugs.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jbia.org/safety6.html|title=日本浴用剤工業会|publisher=日本浴用剤工業会|qq=}}</ref> Quasi-drugs are legally permitted to display effects and efficacy claims similar to those of pharmaceuticals. ==Origin== The history of bath additives dates back thousands of years across various regions. Around 2700 years {{ill|Ante Christum natum|en|Ante Christum natum|qid=Q28200|lt=Before the Common Era|1=Ante Christum natum}}, texts from {{ill|China|de|China|ja|中国|1=China (region)|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|zh|中国|1=China (region)|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q29520}} described multiple types of salts along with extraction and usage methods. The ancient Greek physician {{ill|Hippocrates|en|Hippocrates|qid=Q5264|lt=Hippocrates|1=Hippocrates}} also immersed patients in seawater to treat various ailments and advocated the use of saltwater to his fellow healers. Japan, a country renowned for its abundant hot springs, traces the origins of bath additives to therapeutic baths using natural spring water and medicinal plants.<ref name="osaka"/><ref>{{Citation|title=入浴剤の現状と展望|doi=10.11227/seikisho.56.121|publisher=日本生気象学会|date=2020-02-20|accessdate=2021-10-09|language=ja|qq=}}</ref> ===Derived from Plants and Herbal Medicines=== In ancient Egypt, people bathed with fragrant oils and flowers. In ancient Mexico, descriptions record people of all classes bathing daily with medicinal {{ill|Medicinal plants|en|Medicinal plants|qid=Q188840|lt=herbs|1=Medicinal plants}} for therapeutic purposes.<ref name="autogenerated1">{{cite web|title=…|language=es|url=http://148.206.53.231/UAMI11028.PDF|accessdate=2012-12-18|qq=}}</ref> In Japan, medicinal plant baths ({{ill|herbal baths|de|Kräuterbäder|ja|薬湯|zh|药汤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/herbal baths|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135503410}}) were popular, and during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, they were prescribed to treat skin diseases.<ref name="osaka"/> Traditional customs include adding iris on {{ill|Tango no Sekku|de|Tango-Fest|ja|端午の節句|zh|端午节|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango no Sekku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 端午|1=Dragon Boat Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1254268}} for {{ill|iris baths|de|Schwertlilienbäder|ja|菖蒲湯|zh|菖蒲浴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iris baths|1=iris baths|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619955}} and adding {{ill|yuzu fruit|de|Yuzu-Frucht|ja|柚子|zh|柚子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yuzu fruit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ユズ|1=Yuzu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q867776}} on {{ill|Winter solstice|en|Winter solstice|qid=Q207932|lt=Winter Solstice|1=Winter solstice}} for {{ill|Yuzu bath|en|Yuzu bath|qid=Q11533980|lt=yuzu baths|1=Yuzu bath}}.<ref name="osaka"/> The “Five Woods and Eight Grasses Bath” described in {{ill|Yōjōkun|de|Yōjōkun|ja|養生訓|zh|养生训|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōjōkun|1=Yojokun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11666861}} by {{ill|Kaibara Ekken|en|Kaibara Ekken|qid=Q1721396|lt=Kaibara Ekiken|1=Kaibara Ekken}} lists plants such as [[:en:Mulberry|mulberry]], {{ill|elm|de|Ulme|ja|楡|zh|榆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/elm|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニレ|1=Elm|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q131113}}, {{ill|paulownia|de|Kaiserbaum|ja|桐|zh|泡桐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/paulownia|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to キリ|1=Paulownia tomentosa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q157393}}, iris, and {{ill|honeysuckle|de|Geißblatt|ja|忍冬|zh|忍冬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honeysuckle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スイカズラ|1=Lonicera japonica|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q161083}}.<ref name="osaka"/> Bath specialties at places like {{ill|Daigo Onsen|de|Daigo-Onsen|ja|大子温泉|zh|大子温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daigo Onsen|1=Daigo Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11434220}} may also include local fruits such as apples. By the mid-{{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} these medicinal herbs were packaged in cloth bags as commercial products.<ref name="osaka"/> ===Derived from Hot Spring Components=== Since the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, powdered forms of natural spring components have been commercialized.<ref name="osaka"/> A representative example is {{ill|hot spring deposits|de|Thermalwasserablagerungen|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|zh|汤之花|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring deposits|1=Sinter|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11563426}}. Adding these deposits to baths allows people far from springs to enjoy their benefits. These deposits are collected from {{ill|onsen fields|de|Onsen-Felder|ja|湯畑|1=Yubatake|2=ja|3=湯畑|4=zh|5=汤畑|6=de|7=Onsen-Felder|zh|汤畑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen fields|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubatake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11563768}} or precipitated from alum-rich clay at the {{ill|Beppu Myōban Onsen|de|Beppu Myōban-Onsen|ja|別府の明礬温泉|zh|别府明矾温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Myōban Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503413|1=Beppu Myōban Onsen}} in structures known as Sinter huts. Sulfurous or metal sulfide-rich deposits can damage tubs and should be verified before use. Some manufacturers process spring water from {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|qid=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen|1=Kusatsu Onsen}} or {{ill|Shiraoi Onsen|de|Shiraoi-Onsen|ja|白老温泉|zh|白老温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraoi Onsen|1=Shiraoi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11580321}} into liquid bath additives. Bath additives from Kusatsu are known for turning the water milky white and have been implicated in onsen misrepresentation scandals. Products designed to mimic the effects of {{ill|radium springs|de|Radiumquellen|ja|放射能泉|1=Radioactive spring|zh|放射性温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/radium springs|qq=}} use processed {{ill|radium ore|de|Radiumerz|ja|ラジウム鉱石|zh|镭矿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/radium ore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503414|1=radium ore}} in mesh bags. Unlike hot spring deposits, these can be reused but are expensive and mostly used by {{ill|Sentō|en|Sentō|qid=Q1046648|lt=public bathhouses|1=Sentō}}, {{ill|super sentōs|de|Super-Sentōs|ja|スーパー銭湯|zh|超级公共浴场|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/super sentōs|1=Super Sentō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11313995}}, {{ill|health lands|de|Gesundheitslandschaften|ja|健康ランド|zh|健康乐园|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/health lands|1=Health land|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11387182}}, and inns. Some products hold {{ill|Medication|en|Medication|qid=Q12140|lt=pharmaceutical certification|1=Medication}}. Bath additives marketed as “Onsen no Moto” claim to replicate specific hot spring compositions. ===Derived from Inorganic Mineral Compounds=== The most common domestic bath additives in Japan are made from stable, safe substances found in springs and emerged in the early {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa era|1=Shōwa era}}.<ref name="osaka"/> Initially aimed at users of public baths, since private tubs were rare.<ref name="osaka"/> From the 1960s economic boom and the proliferation of baths in public housing, by {{ill|1980|en|1980|qid=Q2439|lt=1980|1=1980}}, the health trend drove market expansion.<ref name=":0"/> Carbon-dioxide-type and onsen-style bath additives as well as skincare-promoting products were launched around this time.<ref name=":0"/> Powder and tablet forms account for over 80% of the market.<ref name=":0"/> Common ingredients in household powdered types include {{ill|Sodium sulfate|en|Sodium sulfate|qid=Q211737|lt=sodium sulfate|1=Sodium sulfate}}, {{ill|Sodium bicarbonate|en|Sodium bicarbonate|qid=Q179731|lt=sodium bicarbonate|1=Sodium bicarbonate}}, {{ill|Calcium sulfate|en|Calcium sulfate|qid=Q407258|lt=calcium sulfate|1=Calcium sulfate}}, {{ill|Calcium carbonate|en|Calcium carbonate|qid=Q23767|lt=calcium carbonate|1=Calcium carbonate}}, and titanium dioxide. Salts bond with skin proteins to form a film that reduces heat loss and enhances post-bathing warmth. Products containing {{ill|Sodium carbonate|en|Sodium carbonate|qid=Q190227|lt=sodium carbonate|1=Sodium carbonate}} effervesce by releasing carbon dioxide ({{ill|carbonic gas|de|Kohlensäuregas|ja|炭酸ガス|zh|二氧化碳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/carbonic gas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 二酸化炭素|1=Carbon dioxide|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1997}}) when dissolved. Many products now include enzymes and plant extracts for beauty and health effects.<ref name=":0"/> Some products use well-known onsen names purely for image; packaging often notes “This does not replicate onsen water.” True replication would damage tubs due to {{ill|Sulfur|en|Sulfur|qid=Q682|lt=sulfur|1=Sulfur}} content. Many products share the same base ingredients, differing only in dyes and fragrances. Homemade bath additives can be made by mixing powders such as {{ill|Sodium sulfate|en|Sodium sulfate|qid=Q211737|lt=sodium sulfate|1=Sodium sulfate}} and {{ill|Sodium bicarbonate|en|Sodium bicarbonate|qid=Q179731|lt=sodium bicarbonate|1=Sodium bicarbonate}} with {{ill|Essential oil|en|Essential oil|qid=Q170885|lt=essential oils|1=Essential oil}} to create custom products. ==Ingredients of Bath Additives== Homemade and additive ingredients include: ===Liquid Bases=== {{ill|Milk|en|Milk|qid=Q8495|lt=milk|1=Milk}}, {{ill|Soy milk|en|Soy milk|qid=Q192199|lt=soy milk|1=Soy milk}}, {{ill|Alcoholic beverage|en|Alcoholic beverage|qid=Q154|lt=alcohol|1=Alcoholic beverage}} (e.g., {{ill|Wine|en|Wine|qid=Q282|lt=wine|1=Wine}}), {{ill|Tea|en|Tea|qid=Q6097|lt=tea|1=Tea}}, {{ill|Lotion|en|Lotion|qid=Q1871151|lt=lotions|1=Lotion}}, {{ill|rice water|de|Reiswasser|ja|とぎ汁|zh|淘米水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice water|1=rice water|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11273066}}, {{ill|Honey|en|Honey|qid=Q10987|lt=honey|1=Honey}}, baby oil, {{ill|Perfume|en|Perfume|qid=Q131746|lt=perfume|1=Perfume}}, {{ill|fragrant oils|de|Duftöle|ja|香油|zh|香油|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fragrant oils|1=fragrant oils|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18990705}}, {{ill|Vinegar|en|Vinegar|qid=Q41354|lt=vinegar|1=Vinegar}}, {{ill|Polyethylene glycol|en|Polyethylene glycol|qid=Q410083|lt=polyethylene glycol|1=Polyethylene glycol}}, {{ill|Methanol|en|Methanol|qid=Q14982|lt=methanol|1=Methanol}} (methyl alcohol is not used in Japanese bath additives). ===Herbal Bases=== {{ill|peels|de|Schalen|ja|果皮|zh|果皮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/peels|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Pericarp|qid=Q876445}} (e.g., {{ill|Apple|en|Apple|qid=Q89|lt=apple|1=Apple}}, {{ill|Lemon|en|Lemon|qid=Q500|lt=lemon|1=Lemon}}), spices and {{ill|Chinese herbology|en|Chinese herbology|qid=Q261503|lt=herbal medicines|1=Chinese herbology}} (e.g., {{ill|Ginger|en|Ginger|qid=Q35625|lt=ginger|1=Ginger}}, {{ill|Chili pepper|en|Chili pepper|qid=Q165199|lt=chili pepper|1=Chili pepper}}), and {{ill|Herb|en|Herb|qid=Q207123|lt=herbs|1=Herb}} (e.g., {{ill|Perilla frutescens|en|Perilla frutescens|qid=Q1076038|lt=perilla|1=Perilla frutescens}}, {{ill|Peppermint|en|Peppermint|qid=Q156037|lt=peppermint|1=Peppermint}}). ===Solid Bases=== {{ill|table salt|de|Kochsalz|ja|食塩|zh|食盐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/table salt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 塩|1=Salt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11254}}, {{ill|Bath salts|en|Bath salts|qid=Q798972|lt=bath salts|1=Bath salts}}, {{ill|baking soda|de|Natron|ja|重曹|zh|小苏打|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/baking soda|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 炭酸水素ナトリウム|1=Sodium bicarbonate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q179731}}, {{ill|Citric acid|en|Citric acid|qid=Q159683|lt=citric acid|1=Citric acid}}, and {{ill|hot spring deposits|de|Thermalwasserablagerungen|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|zh|汤之花|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring deposits|1=Sinter|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11563426}}. ==References== {{Reflist|qq=}} ==See also== * {{ill|Aromatherapy|en|Aromatherapy|qid=Q206202|lt=aromatherapy|1=Aromatherapy}} * {{ill|Balneotherapy|en|Balneotherapy|qid=Q2016490|lt=hydrotherapy|1=Balneotherapy}} * {{ill|Bath fizzies|en|Bath fizzies|qid=Q4868974|lt=Bath fizzies|1=Bath fizzies}} * {{ill|Bath bomb|en|Bath bomb|qid=Q2869238|lt=Bath bomb|1=Bath Additive}} ;Manufacturers * [https://store.chapon.jp/ Chapon – Bath Additive Gift Specialist] * {{ill|Bathclin|de|Bathclin|ja|バスクリン|zh|巴斯克林|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bathclin|1=Bathclin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11327654}} * {{ill|Hakugen Earth|de|Hakugen Earth|ja|白元アース|zh|白元地球|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakugen Earth|1=Hakugen Earth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18338809}} * {{ill|Kao Corporation|en|Kao Corporation|qid=Q902855|lt=Kao Corporation|1=Kao Corporation}} – {{ill|Bub|de|Bub|ja|バブ|zh|巴布|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bub|1=Bub|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11328007}} * {{ill|Earth Corporation|de|Earth Corporation|ja|アース製薬|zh|地球制药|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earth Corporation|1=Earth Chemical|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11285616}} – {{ill|Bath Roman|de|Bath Roman|ja|バスロマン|zh|泡澡罗曼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bath Roman|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135503417}} * {{ill|onsen no moto.com|de|Onsen no Moto.com|ja|温泉の素.com|zh|温泉之素.com|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen no moto.com|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503418|1=onsennomoto.com}} * {{ill|Young Venus Pharmaceutical|de|Young Venus Pharmaceutical|ja|ヤングビーナス薬品工業|zh|年轻金星药业|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Young Venus Pharmaceutical|1=Young Venus Pharmaceutical|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11345437}} – {{ill|Yōmeisen|de|Yōmeisen|ja|養命泉|zh|养命泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōmeisen|1=Yōmeisen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11666817}}, {{ill|Young Venus|de|Young Venus|ja|ヤングビーナス|zh|年轻维纳斯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Young Venus|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135503420}} * {{ill|Muto Seiyaku|de|Muto Seiyaku|ja|武藤鉦製薬|zh|武藤铿制药|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muto Seiyaku|1=Mutoshō Seiyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11546533}} – {{ill|610 Hop|de|610 Hop|ja|六一〇ハップ|zh|六一〇哈普|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/610 Hop|1=muto hap|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11392263}} * {{ill|Asahi Shōnō|de|Asahi Sōnō|ja|アサヒ晶脳|zh|旭日晶脑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Shōnō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135503423}} – {{ill|Bath Melody|de|Bath Melody|ja|バスメロディー|zh|浴曲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bath Melody|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135503424}} ==External links== * [http://www.jbia.org/ Japan Bath Additive Industry Association]
{{translated page|ja|入浴剤|version=103983820|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ko:%EC%9E%85%EC%9A%95%EC%A0%9C]] [[ja:入浴剤]] [[de:Badezusätze]] [[Category:Pages with duplicated content]] ==Merged second translation==
[[File:Lush_bath_bombs.jpg|thumb|right]] '''Nyūyokuzai''' (入浴剤) are substances added to bathwater during {{ill|Bathing|en|Bathing|qid=Q327651|lt=bathing|1=Bathing}} in a {{ill|Furo|en|Furo|qid=Q2637646|lt=bathtub|1=Furo}}. These are prepared bath products, such as fragrant bath salts, intended for {{ill|Bathing|en|Bathing|qid=Q327651|lt=bathing|1=Bathing}}.<ref name="osaka">{{Cite web|language=ja|url=https://www.customs.go.jp/osaka/toukei/pdf/tokushu_201004.pdf|title=入浴剤の輸入|publisher=大阪税関|accessdate=2019-10-20|qq=}}</ref> They come in various forms, including {{ill|Solid|en|Solid|qid=Q11438|lt=solids|1=Solid}}, {{ill|Powder|en|Powder|qid=Q2908004|lt=powders|1=Powder}} and {{ill|Liquid|en|Liquid|qid=Q11435|lt=liquids|1=Liquid}}. Bath additives can be broadly categorized into three types: those derived from natural {{ill|Plant|en|Plant|qid=Q756|lt=plants|1=Plant}} or {{ill|Chinese herbology|en|Chinese herbology|qid=Q261503|lt=herbal medicines|1=Chinese herbology}}, extracts of {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|qid=Q655311|lt=hot spring water|1=Onsen}} components, and inorganic {{ill|mineral salts|de|Mineralsalze|ja|無機塩類|zh|无机盐类|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mineral salts|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミネラル|1=Mineral (nutrient)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q189598}} compounds. Combinations of these types are also available. In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, bath additives are regulated as either {{ill|Cosmetics|en|Cosmetics|qid=Q131207|lt=cosmetics|1=Cosmetics}} (bathing cosmetics), {{ill|quasi-drugs|de|Arzneimittel in der Übergangsphase|ja|医薬部外品|zh|准药品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/quasi-drugs|1=quasi-drug|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11404705}}, or {{ill|Medication|en|Medication|qid=Q12140|lt=pharmaceuticals|1=Medication}}. Most products on the market are classified as quasi-drugs.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jbia.org/safety6.html|title=日本浴用剤工業会|publisher=日本浴用剤工業会|qq=}}</ref> Quasi-drugs are legally permitted to display effects and efficacy claims similar to those of pharmaceuticals. ==Origin== The history of bath additives dates back thousands of years across various regions. Around 2700 years {{ill|Ante Christum natum|en|Ante Christum natum|qid=Q28200|lt=Before the Common Era|1=Ante Christum natum}}, texts from {{ill|China|de|China|ja|中国|1=China (region)|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|zh|中国|1=China (region)|2=ja|3=中国|lt=China|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q29520}} described multiple types of salts along with extraction and usage methods. The ancient Greek physician {{ill|Hippocrates|en|Hippocrates|qid=Q5264|lt=Hippocrates|1=Hippocrates}} also immersed patients in seawater to treat various ailments and advocated the use of saltwater to his fellow healers. Japan, a country renowned for its abundant hot springs, traces the origins of bath additives to therapeutic baths using natural spring water and medicinal plants.<ref name="osaka"/><ref>{{Citation|title=入浴剤の現状と展望|doi=10.11227/seikisho.56.121|publisher=日本生気象学会|date=2020-02-20|accessdate=2021-10-09|language=ja|qq=}}</ref> ===Derived from Plants and Herbal Medicines=== In ancient Egypt, people bathed with fragrant oils and flowers. In ancient Mexico, descriptions record people of all classes bathing daily with medicinal {{ill|Medicinal plants|en|Medicinal plants|qid=Q188840|lt=herbs|1=Medicinal plants}} for therapeutic purposes.<ref name="autogenerated1">{{cite web|title=…|language=es|url=http://148.206.53.231/UAMI11028.PDF|accessdate=2012-12-18|qq=}}</ref> In Japan, medicinal plant baths ({{ill|herbal baths|de|Kräuterbäder|ja|薬湯|zh|药汤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/herbal baths|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135503410}}) were popular, and during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, they were prescribed to treat skin diseases.<ref name="osaka"/> Traditional customs include adding iris on {{ill|Tango no Sekku|de|Tango-Fest|ja|端午の節句|zh|端午节|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tango no Sekku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 端午|1=Dragon Boat Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1254268}} for {{ill|iris baths|de|Schwertlilienbäder|ja|菖蒲湯|zh|菖蒲浴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iris baths|1=iris baths|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619955}} and adding {{ill|yuzu fruit|de|Yuzu-Frucht|ja|柚子|zh|柚子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yuzu fruit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ユズ|1=Yuzu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q867776}} on {{ill|Winter solstice|en|Winter solstice|qid=Q207932|lt=Winter Solstice|1=Winter solstice}} for {{ill|Yuzu bath|en|Yuzu bath|qid=Q11533980|lt=yuzu baths|1=Yuzu bath}}.<ref name="osaka"/> The “Five Woods and Eight Grasses Bath” described in {{ill|Yōjōkun|de|Yōjōkun|ja|養生訓|zh|养生训|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōjōkun|1=Yojokun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11666861}} by {{ill|Kaibara Ekken|en|Kaibara Ekken|qid=Q1721396|lt=Kaibara Ekiken|1=Kaibara Ekken}} lists plants such as [[:en:Mulberry|mulberry]], {{ill|elm|de|Ulme|ja|楡|zh|榆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/elm|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ニレ|1=Elm|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q131113}}, {{ill|paulownia|de|Kaiserbaum|ja|桐|zh|泡桐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/paulownia|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to キリ|1=Paulownia tomentosa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q157393}}, iris, and {{ill|honeysuckle|de|Geißblatt|ja|忍冬|zh|忍冬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honeysuckle|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スイカズラ|1=Lonicera japonica|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q161083}}.<ref name="osaka"/> Bath specialties at places like {{ill|Daigo Onsen|de|Daigo-Onsen|ja|大子温泉|zh|大子温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daigo Onsen|1=Daigo Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11434220}} may also include local fruits such as apples. By the mid-{{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} these medicinal herbs were packaged in cloth bags as commercial products.<ref name="osaka"/> ===Derived from Hot Spring Components=== Since the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, powdered forms of natural spring components have been commercialized.<ref name="osaka"/> A representative example is {{ill|hot spring deposits|de|Thermalwasserablagerungen|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|zh|汤之花|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring deposits|1=Sinter|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11563426}}. Adding these deposits to baths allows people far from springs to enjoy their benefits. These deposits are collected from {{ill|onsen fields|de|Onsen-Felder|ja|湯畑|1=Yubatake|2=ja|3=湯畑|4=zh|5=汤畑|6=de|7=Onsen-Felder|zh|汤畑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen fields|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubatake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11563768}} or precipitated from alum-rich clay at the {{ill|Beppu Myōban Onsen|de|Beppu Myōban-Onsen|ja|別府の明礬温泉|zh|别府明矾温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beppu Myōban Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503413|1=Beppu Myōban Onsen}} in structures known as Sinter huts. Sulfurous or metal sulfide-rich deposits can damage tubs and should be verified before use. Some manufacturers process spring water from {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|qid=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen|1=Kusatsu Onsen}} or {{ill|Shiraoi Onsen|de|Shiraoi-Onsen|ja|白老温泉|zh|白老温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiraoi Onsen|1=Shiraoi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11580321}} into liquid bath additives. Bath additives from Kusatsu are known for turning the water milky white and have been implicated in onsen misrepresentation scandals. Products designed to mimic the effects of {{ill|radium springs|de|Radiumquellen|ja|放射能泉|1=Radioactive spring|zh|放射性温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/radium springs|qq=}} use processed {{ill|radium ore|de|Radiumerz|ja|ラジウム鉱石|zh|镭矿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/radium ore|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503414|1=radium ore}} in mesh bags. Unlike hot spring deposits, these can be reused but are expensive and mostly used by {{ill|Sentō|en|Sentō|qid=Q1046648|lt=public bathhouses|1=Sentō}}, {{ill|super sentōs|de|Super-Sentōs|ja|スーパー銭湯|zh|超级公共浴场|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/super sentōs|1=Super Sentō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11313995}}, {{ill|health lands|de|Gesundheitslandschaften|ja|健康ランド|zh|健康乐园|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/health lands|1=Health land|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11387182}}, and inns. Some products hold {{ill|Medication|en|Medication|qid=Q12140|lt=pharmaceutical certification|1=Medication}}. Bath additives marketed as “Onsen no Moto” claim to replicate specific hot spring compositions. ===Derived from Inorganic Mineral Compounds=== The most common domestic bath additives in Japan are made from stable, safe substances found in springs and emerged in the early {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa era|1=Shōwa era}}.<ref name="osaka"/> Initially aimed at users of public baths, since private tubs were rare.<ref name="osaka"/> From the 1960s economic boom and the proliferation of baths in public housing, by {{ill|1980|en|1980|qid=Q2439|lt=1980|1=1980}}, the health trend drove market expansion.<ref name=":0"/> Carbon-dioxide-type and onsen-style bath additives as well as skincare-promoting products were launched around this time.<ref name=":0"/> Powder and tablet forms account for over 80% of the market.<ref name=":0"/> Common ingredients in household powdered types include {{ill|Sodium sulfate|en|Sodium sulfate|qid=Q211737|lt=sodium sulfate|1=Sodium sulfate}}, {{ill|Sodium bicarbonate|en|Sodium bicarbonate|qid=Q179731|lt=sodium bicarbonate|1=Sodium bicarbonate}}, {{ill|Calcium sulfate|en|Calcium sulfate|qid=Q407258|lt=calcium sulfate|1=Calcium sulfate}}, {{ill|Calcium carbonate|en|Calcium carbonate|qid=Q23767|lt=calcium carbonate|1=Calcium carbonate}}, and titanium dioxide. Salts bond with skin proteins to form a film that reduces heat loss and enhances post-bathing warmth. Products containing {{ill|Sodium carbonate|en|Sodium carbonate|qid=Q190227|lt=sodium carbonate|1=Sodium carbonate}} effervesce by releasing carbon dioxide ({{ill|carbonic gas|de|Kohlensäuregas|ja|炭酸ガス|zh|二氧化碳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/carbonic gas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 二酸化炭素|1=Carbon dioxide|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1997}}) when dissolved. Many products now include enzymes and plant extracts for beauty and health effects.<ref name=":0"/> Some products use well-known onsen names purely for image; packaging often notes “This does not replicate onsen water.” True replication would damage tubs due to {{ill|Sulfur|en|Sulfur|qid=Q682|lt=sulfur|1=Sulfur}} content. Many products share the same base ingredients, differing only in dyes and fragrances. Homemade bath additives can be made by mixing powders such as {{ill|Sodium sulfate|en|Sodium sulfate|qid=Q211737|lt=sodium sulfate|1=Sodium sulfate}} and {{ill|Sodium bicarbonate|en|Sodium bicarbonate|qid=Q179731|lt=sodium bicarbonate|1=Sodium bicarbonate}} with {{ill|Essential oil|en|Essential oil|qid=Q170885|lt=essential oils|1=Essential oil}} to create custom products. ==Ingredients of Bath Additives== Homemade and additive ingredients include: ===Liquid Bases=== {{ill|Milk|en|Milk|qid=Q8495|lt=milk|1=Milk}}, {{ill|Soy milk|en|Soy milk|qid=Q192199|lt=soy milk|1=Soy milk}}, {{ill|Alcoholic beverage|en|Alcoholic beverage|qid=Q154|lt=alcohol|1=Alcoholic beverage}} (e.g., {{ill|Wine|en|Wine|qid=Q282|lt=wine|1=Wine}}), {{ill|Tea|en|Tea|qid=Q6097|lt=tea|1=Tea}}, {{ill|Lotion|en|Lotion|qid=Q1871151|lt=lotions|1=Lotion}}, {{ill|rice water|de|Reiswasser|ja|とぎ汁|zh|淘米水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice water|1=rice water|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11273066}}, {{ill|Honey|en|Honey|qid=Q10987|lt=honey|1=Honey}}, baby oil, {{ill|Perfume|en|Perfume|qid=Q131746|lt=perfume|1=Perfume}}, {{ill|fragrant oils|de|Duftöle|ja|香油|zh|香油|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fragrant oils|1=fragrant oils|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18990705}}, {{ill|Vinegar|en|Vinegar|qid=Q41354|lt=vinegar|1=Vinegar}}, {{ill|Polyethylene glycol|en|Polyethylene glycol|qid=Q410083|lt=polyethylene glycol|1=Polyethylene glycol}}, {{ill|Methanol|en|Methanol|qid=Q14982|lt=methanol|1=Methanol}} (methyl alcohol is not used in Japanese bath additives). ===Herbal Bases=== {{ill|peels|de|Schalen|ja|果皮|zh|果皮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/peels|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Pericarp|qid=Q876445}} (e.g., {{ill|Apple|en|Apple|qid=Q89|lt=apple|1=Apple}}, {{ill|Lemon|en|Lemon|qid=Q500|lt=lemon|1=Lemon}}), spices and {{ill|Chinese herbology|en|Chinese herbology|qid=Q261503|lt=herbal medicines|1=Chinese herbology}} (e.g., {{ill|Ginger|en|Ginger|qid=Q35625|lt=ginger|1=Ginger}}, {{ill|Chili pepper|en|Chili pepper|qid=Q165199|lt=chili pepper|1=Chili pepper}}), and {{ill|Herb|en|Herb|qid=Q207123|lt=herbs|1=Herb}} (e.g., {{ill|Perilla frutescens|en|Perilla frutescens|qid=Q1076038|lt=perilla|1=Perilla frutescens}}, {{ill|Peppermint|en|Peppermint|qid=Q156037|lt=peppermint|1=Peppermint}}). ===Solid Bases=== {{ill|table salt|de|Kochsalz|ja|食塩|zh|食盐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/table salt|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 塩|1=Salt|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11254}}, {{ill|Bath salts|en|Bath salts|qid=Q798972|lt=bath salts|1=Bath salts}}, {{ill|baking soda|de|Natron|ja|重曹|zh|小苏打|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/baking soda|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 炭酸水素ナトリウム|1=Sodium bicarbonate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q179731}}, {{ill|Citric acid|en|Citric acid|qid=Q159683|lt=citric acid|1=Citric acid}}, and {{ill|hot spring deposits|de|Thermalwasserablagerungen|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|zh|汤之花|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hot spring deposits|1=Sinter|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11563426}}. ==References== {{Reflist|qq=}} ==See also== * {{ill|Aromatherapy|en|Aromatherapy|qid=Q206202|lt=aromatherapy|1=Aromatherapy}} * {{ill|Balneotherapy|en|Balneotherapy|qid=Q2016490|lt=hydrotherapy|1=Balneotherapy}} * {{ill|Bath fizzies|en|Bath fizzies|qid=Q4868974|lt=Bath fizzies|1=Bath fizzies}} * {{ill|Bath bomb|en|Bath bomb|qid=Q2869238|lt=Bath bomb|1=Bath Additive}} ;Manufacturers * [https://store.chapon.jp/ Chapon – Bath Additive Gift Specialist] * {{ill|Bathclin|de|Bathclin|ja|バスクリン|zh|巴斯克林|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bathclin|1=Bathclin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11327654}} * {{ill|Hakugen Earth|de|Hakugen Earth|ja|白元アース|zh|白元地球|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakugen Earth|1=Hakugen Earth|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18338809}} * {{ill|Kao Corporation|en|Kao Corporation|qid=Q902855|lt=Kao Corporation|1=Kao Corporation}} – {{ill|Bub|de|Bub|ja|バブ|zh|巴布|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bub|1=Bub|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11328007}} * {{ill|Earth Corporation|de|Earth Corporation|ja|アース製薬|zh|地球制药|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Earth Corporation|1=Earth Chemical|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11285616}} – {{ill|Bath Roman|de|Bath Roman|ja|バスロマン|zh|泡澡罗曼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bath Roman|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135503417}} * {{ill|onsen no moto.com|de|Onsen no Moto.com|ja|温泉の素.com|zh|温泉之素.com|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/onsen no moto.com|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503418|1=onsennomoto.com}} * {{ill|Young Venus Pharmaceutical|de|Young Venus Pharmaceutical|ja|ヤングビーナス薬品工業|zh|年轻金星药业|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Young Venus Pharmaceutical|1=Young Venus Pharmaceutical|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11345437}} – {{ill|Yōmeisen|de|Yōmeisen|ja|養命泉|zh|养命泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōmeisen|1=Yōmeisen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11666817}}, {{ill|Young Venus|de|Young Venus|ja|ヤングビーナス|zh|年轻维纳斯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Young Venus|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135503420}} * {{ill|Muto Seiyaku|de|Muto Seiyaku|ja|武藤鉦製薬|zh|武藤铿制药|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muto Seiyaku|1=Mutoshō Seiyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11546533}} – {{ill|610 Hop|de|610 Hop|ja|六一〇ハップ|zh|六一〇哈普|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/610 Hop|1=muto hap|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11392263}} * {{ill|Asahi Shōnō|de|Asahi Sōnō|ja|アサヒ晶脳|zh|旭日晶脑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi Shōnō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135503423}} – {{ill|Bath Melody|de|Bath Melody|ja|バスメロディー|zh|浴曲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bath Melody|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135503424}} ==External links== * [http://www.jbia.org/ Japan Bath Additive Industry Association]
